comments.text: My student Junzhe Mao and postdoc Kyle Yip had worked on this problem some, and then Junzhe made an advance on it, and we have a "proof from the book" upper bound. This and other results will be included in a forthcoming paper we are writing. Below is a version of the argument, which was heavily inspired by Brandon Hanson's paper "Additive Correlation and the Inverse Problem for the Large Sieve". We didn't use AI for this work. Basically, we have an upper bound of the form: $$ |A|\ <\ N \exp \left ( - c {\log N \over \log\log N}\right ), $$ for some $c > 0$. Proof: We can assume $|A| > N^{1-\varepsilon}$, or else we are done. Let $r_{A-A}(n)$ denote the number of ordered pairs $a,a'\in A$, $a \neq a'$, such that $a-a' =n$. Note that if $A$ is a Sidon set, it implies that $r_{A-A}(n) = 0$ or $1$. Let $d$ be an integer that is a product of about $t = (1-2\varepsilon)(\log N)/ \log\log N$ primes in $[\log N, 2 \log N]$; and note that the Prime Number Theorem implies $d < N^{1-2\varepsilon+o(1)}$, so $|A|>d$. Let $A(r,d)$ denote the set of elements of $A$ that are $r$ mod $d$. Now, $A$ is contained in the squares, and since the squares occupy $(p+1)/2$ residue classes mod $p$, for each prime $p \geq 3$, we have that $A$ can occupy at most $d(1+o(1)) / 2^t$ residue classes mod $d$. Using Cauchy-Schwarz we have $$ \sum_{|n| \leq N^2 \atop d|n} r_{A-A}(n) = \left ( \sum_{r=0}^{d-1} |A(r,d)|^2 \right ) - |A| \gg {2^t |A|^2 \over d} - |A| > {2^{t-1} |A|^2 \over d}, $$ where the last inequality follows from the fact that $|A|>d$. On the other hand, since $A$ is Sidon (implying $r_{A-A}(n) =0$ or $1$, recall), the first sum is bounded from above by about $2N^2/d$. So, we get $$ {2N^2 \over d} \gg {2^{t-1} |A|^2 \over d}, $$ and the result follows.
Thanks Ernie, and welcome to the site! Great result, I've always liked this problem. Do you have a feeling what the truth is (I've always thought it surely must be $N^{1-o(1)}$)

I think the upper bound ought to be $M^{\theta}$ for some $\theta < 1/2$, but I don't have a good feeling for the exact $\theta$. We (Junzhe and Kyle and I) have discussed this a few times. Or, using the $N$ notation, $N^{\alpha}$ for some $\alpha < 1$.

Just thought I would mention one more thing. See this paper (Theorem 1.2): https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1002/rsa.20496 If the squares in $[N^2]$ behave like random sets of size $N$, then one would expect that the largest Sidon set contained within it has size about $N^{2/3 + o(1)}$. If so, then it means that improving the lower bound by finding a better construction is a fool's errand; and that what one *should* be doing is trying to improve the upper bound.
I think, Lefmann and Thiele back in 1995 [LeTh95] had also thought in this direction.

I agree that if the squares act completely randomly this is the threshold, but I'd guess/hope that the squares do not act like a random set from this perspective. Compare to the question of the largest Sidon subset of $\{1,\ldots,N\}$ - a purely random construction only produces Sidon sets of size up to $\approx N^{1/3}$, but of course we know various clever algebraic constructions that do much better, $\sim N^{1/2}$. I see no reason not to expect some similar clever algebraic trick that creates a large Sidon, that now interacts well enough with squares to work in this context.

I'd personally put 20% effort on improving the lower bound and 80% on the upper bound. It's possible that the squares may "play nice" with some magic algebraic construction, but I'm pessimistic. The random model in that paper, by the way, does take into account magic sets like this, since it's the outer sets that are randomly chosen, and then inner set that gives the largest Sidon set is the one whose size it tries to predict -- and this one matches algebraic constructions. It would interesting to try to come up with some nice algebraic or interesting quasi-algebraic set where the largest Sidon set inside it is larger than the random model would predict for a set of similar density -- in case the lower bound on #773 can be improved, yet the improvement is very hard to find. e.g. maybe something like the set of numbers that are the sum of two 4th powers, which seems like it might be close enough to being Sidon that all you need is a small tweak.

Cosmin Pohoata and Adam Sheffer use this to say some new things about distinct distances in a grid and also about sets of grid points not containing an isosceles triangle: https://pohoatza.wordpress.com/2026/04/27/sets-with-no-isosceles-triangles-repeated-distances-in-atlanta/

We have $g(\{1, 2, 3, 4\}) = 4$, while $g(\{1, 4, 7, 8\}) = 3$ because $1^2 + 8^2 = 4^2 + 7^2$. So (as stated) the inequality $g(A) \ge g(\{1, 2, \ldots, N \})$ cannot uniformly hold for all $N$ and all sets $A$ with $|A| = N$. Perhaps a weaker version of the inequality does hold, not sure.
Yes, I assume he was likely thinking of an asymptotic version of this inequality.
I submitted A390813. Draft edits here (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
